name: Bia
description: Bia, in Greek mythology, is the personification of force, power, might, and compulsion. She is one of the four winged children of the Titan Pallas and Styx, along with her siblings Nike (Victory), Kratos (Strength), and Zelus (Zeal). All four siblings are closely associated with Zeus, having sided with him during the Titanomachy, the great war between the Titans and the Olympian gods. Bia is often depicted as a powerful and unwavering figure, embodying the irresistible strength and enforcement of Zeus's will. Her role is to ensure that his commands are carried out without question, and she is a constant reminder of the consequences of defying the king of the gods. She is a symbol of the raw, unyielding power that underpins the Olympian order, and her presence signifies the absolute authority of Zeus. Bia is not typically worshipped in temples or shrines, but her influence is felt in the execution of justice and the enforcement of divine law. Her power is inextricably linked to that of Zeus, and she is a vital component of his reign. She embodies the harsh reality that power dictates order, and that defiance will be met with swift and inescapable force. Bia's unwavering loyalty and embodiment of force serve as a constant reminder of Zeus's dominance and the consequences of challenging his authority.
subtitle: Goddess of Force and Compulsion

keyMyths.title: Alliance with Zeus
keyMyths.description: Bia and her siblings sided with Zeus in the Titanomachy, granting him the power needed to overthrow the Titans.

keyMyths.title: Binding of Prometheus
keyMyths.description: Bia, along with Kratos, was commanded by Zeus to bind Prometheus to a rock as punishment for giving fire to humanity.

keyMyths.title: Enforcement of Divine Law
keyMyths.description: Bia represents the unyielding force that ensures Zeus's commands and divine laws are carried out without question.

sources.author: Hesiod
